title: Easy v0 saturates the model — benchmark is the bottleneck
finding: Frozen Qwen3.5-4B scores F1 ~98-100 on v0 with recall 100%. No headroom to measure any SFT/CoT/OPD delta; differences would be lost in noise.
suggestions: Add hard families (counterfactual, intent-only, near-boundary, obfuscation) before training.

title: Regex is a clean high-precision / low-recall foil
finding: Regex baseline: P~95 R~30 F1~45. Catches secret_credential & infra_config (literal secrets) but is 0% on access_control / security_policy / vuln_incident.
suggestions: Keep regex as a deterministic pre-filter for literal-secret subcategories; rely on the model for semantic intent.

title: Counterfactual pairs are the entire difficulty signal
finding: v1 drops model F1 to 96.2. All errors are engineered boundaries: over-triggers on access REQUESTS (vs grants) and third-party breach NEWS (vs our incident). Counterfactual slice specificity ~58%.
suggestions: Train with CoT that explicitly reasons: is this ENACTED? is this US? before labeling.
Push counterfactual volume/variety for a steeper curve.

title: Model already robust to obfuscation and intent-only disclosure
finding: Negative result: obfuscation (base64/spaced secrets) and intent-only positives (no literal secret) both score 100%. These families add coverage but not difficulty.
suggestions: Do not over-invest in obfuscation/intent-only; concentrate hard-data effort on counterfactual + near-boundary.

title: Report specificity on all-negative slices (eval honesty)
finding: near_boundary is all-negative, so P/R/F1 are degenerate (F1=0 looked like failure). Its true performance is 94.4% specificity.
suggestions: Per-slice now shows support (pos count) + specificity; use specificity for negative-heavy slices.

title: Over-triggering is the dominant failure mode; new pair types isolate it
finding: v2 model: recall 100%, all 7 errors are false positives. The model fires on near-boundary negatives that share surface features with positives: the AWS docs EXAMPLE key (twice), access REQUESTS/proposals (not enacted), synthetic test PII (000-00-0000 fixtures), and third-party breach NEWS.
suggestions: Primary training objective should reduce false positives: teach 'is it real / enacted / us?' Reasoning (CoT) likely helps most here.
Pick a precision-favoring operating point for IT (FP fatigue) once we can tune a threshold.

title: Slice F1 is not comparable across versions when the slice content changes
finding: Model is unchanged (frozen Qwen3.5-4B) between v1 and v2, so every metric delta is due to DATA composition, not model skill. Counterfactual F1 'rose' 78.6->88.9 only because v2 added some easier pair types alongside harder ones. The Versions ΔF1 measures benchmark change here, not model improvement.
suggestions: When reading version diffs, hold the model fixed OR the data fixed — not both. Use frozen-model diffs to gauge benchmark difficulty; use fixed-data diffs to gauge training gains.

title: Harder counterfactuals open ~7pts of headroom; FP profile is crisp
finding: Frozen model F1 95.8->93.3 (CI 89-97), recall still 100%. The 12 FPs cluster: documented example keys (AWS docs example x4, Stripe test key), third-party breach NEWS x4, plus the new expired/dead key, revoke-proposal, synthetic test PII, and last-4 card. Regex precision also fell to 81% (it flags the example keys too).
suggestions: Two clean training targets remain: (a) example/placeholder secrets that look real, (b) third-party news vs our incident. CoT 'is it real / ours / enacted?' should hit both.

title: Rubric call: explicitly dead/revoked credentials labeled PASS
finding: Deliberate, documented choice (cf_expired_vs_live): a credential stated to be rotated-out/revoked grants nothing, so it's labeled negative; the live one is positive. The frozen model currently over-triggers on the dead key (fires on the AKIA shape regardless of 'dead now' context). This is a defensible-but-contestable boundary worth flagging to reviewers.
suggestions: If reviewers prefer treating any key-shaped string as sensitive, flip these labels and regenerate; the generator makes it a one-line change.

title: Real eval exposes a synthetic->real gap AND a labeling bug (CVE)
finding: Combined eval: synthetic F1 94.9 vs REAL F1 41.0. But it decomposes: 91 of 107 real false-positives are CVE advisories (security_news spec 2.2%). Excluding CVE, real F1=76.9 / spec=93.8 — the model transfers reasonably to real negatives (code 99%, ticket 98%, support 97% spec); residual gap is precision on PII prose (spec 74%).
suggestions: Don't read 'F1 41' as pure model failure — slice it. The model's real weakness is moderate over-triggering on PII prose, not collapse.

title: RUBRIC DECISION NEEDED: are third-party CVE advisories in or out of scope?
finding: I mapped CVE -> security_news -> NEGATIVE, but the IT policy lists 'vulnerability/incident details' as IN-SCOPE. The model flags 91/93 CVEs as TRIGGER — arguably agreeing with the policy, not my label. This single choice dominates the real headline metric. It also contradicts my synthetic cf_cve_advisory pair (CVE-about-unused-lib -> negative), so the rubric is currently inconsistent.
suggestions: Decide: (a) CVE advisories = POSITIVE (vulnerability details, in-scope) — recommended for a safety-first IT flag; reserve 'security_news' negative for pure commentary ('Company X breached, analysts say'); (b) keep negative because a PUBLISHED CVE isn't a sensitive disclosure; (c) drop CVE as ambiguous. Then realign synthetic cf_cve_advisory to match.

title: CVE dropped -> clean synthetic->real gap is ~25 F1 (94.9 -> 70.0), and genuine
finding: With CVE removed, real spec is healthy (93.5%); the gap is now real model behavior, not a label artifact. Two drivers: (1) over-triggering on casual-PII PROSE (pii_handling spec 71%) — names/emails in ordinary text we label negative; (2) lower RECALL on real positives (80% vs 100% synthetic) — real breach tickets / PII-handling are phrased unlike our templates, so the model misses some. Code/support/helpdesk real negatives are ~98-100% spec.
suggestions: Training target shifts: synthetic over-triggers (FP), real also UNDER-triggers (FN) on out-of-distribution phrasing. A small real slice in TRAIN should help recall; CoT should help PII-prose precision.
PII-prose spec is partly label ambiguity (is a name/email in prose 'PII handling'?) — tighten the rubric for low-sensitivity PII.

title: Casual register + typos expose typo-brittleness (and close part of the realism gap)
finding: Added a casual long-form family (Slack/Reddit PSAs, vents, rambling disclosures) and a noise injector (40% of rows, secret/number spans protected). New slice 'noisy': clean rows F1 95.6/spec 89.4 vs noisy F1 88.9/spec 74.3 — the model over-triggers ~15pts more on misspelled negatives (recall stays 100%). Casual positives (buried secrets/PII/access) were caught fine; the PSA-style casual negatives are a clean new hard negative.
suggestions: Train with noise augmentation (the generator does this for free) to harden against typos — likely lifts real-data specificity too, since real text is messy.
Keep noise OFF secret spans (already enforced) so regex + secret labels stay valid.

title: SFT solves the engineered weaknesses; fixes real under-triggering
finding: First SFT (LoRA r32, 2 epochs, synthetic+real, noise-augmented) vs frozen baseline, SAME held-out test (valid model-changed/data-fixed comparison). Synthetic F1 92.8->99.4, real F1 70->73.7. Counterfactual specificity 47.6->95.2 and noisy specificity 74->100 (noise aug worked, per f013). Real recall 80->100 (under-triggering on OOD phrasing fixed), recall now 100% everywhere (0 FN).
suggestions: Next: CoT SFT should target the remaining real PII-prose over-triggering (prose spec 55%). Also tighten the casual/low-sensitivity PII rubric — some of those 'FPs' are debatable labels.
Watch for overfitting to the label format (train NLL ~0.005); CoT and OPD are the levers for the harder real precision.

title: ABLATION: prompted CoT (templated rationales) does NOT help and hurts real precision
finding: On ds-v4, SFT+CoT vs SFT(no-CoT): overall F1 90.6->84.6, real F1 73.7->60.2, real precision 58->46, pii spec 57->45, recall 100->96.8 — at ~11x completion tokens (1.1k->12k) and +37% cost. Synthetic gains hold (98.4; counterfactual/noisy still solved). Failure mode: the model emits the templated rationale 'discloses sensitive PII -> TRIGGER' on any PII-shaped real text (names/phones/DOBs), i.e. it pattern-matched the rationale rather than learning high- vs low-sensitivity discrimination.
suggestions: For this short-classification task, no-CoT SFT is the better, cheaper choice — CoT is not worth its latency/cost here. Keep SFT as the production candidate.
The real fix for PII precision is upstream: tighten the low-sensitivity-PII rubric (records with name+DOB+phone+address are debatably positive). Several CoT 'FPs' are label-ambiguous.
If revisiting CoT, use a stronger TEACHER to generate genuinely discriminative rationales (-> stage 3 OPD), not templated ones.

title: PII rubric fix (ds-v5): ~half the real-precision gap was a labeling artifact
finding: Relabeled ai4privacy so multi-identifier personal RECORDS (>=3 identifiers) are positive PII-handling, not just HIGH_SENS rows. Same SFT model (trained on ds-v4) evaluated on the corrected ds-v5: real F1 73.7->81.0, real precision 58->70 — purely from fixing labels, model unchanged. Confirms f015: a big chunk of the 'real precision problem' was mislabeled records the model was correctly flagging.
suggestions: Treat the rubric fix as the real win here; ds-v5 (SFT v1) is the production candidate.

title: Retraining on corrected labels did NOT beat the label-fix alone (fuzzy PII boundary)
finding: SFT v2 (retrained on ds-v5) real F1 74.4 / precision 60.4 — slightly WORSE than SFT v1 evaluated on ds-v5 (81.0 / 69.6), within the wide CI of ~33 real positives. Training records=positive made the model over-generalize PII->trigger (+7 real FPs) on the residual negatives (lone identifiers), which sit right against the record boundary. The residual real-PII precision (pii spec ~52) is a genuine fuzzy-threshold problem, not fixable by this rubric tweak or retraining alone.
suggestions: Accept the residual as inherent label ambiguity; don't chase it with more SFT. If it matters for production, define an explicit identifier-count threshold WITH stakeholders and/or route borderline PII to human review.

title: OPD teacher-ceiling: the larger Qwen (235B) zero-shot is WORSE than our SFT-4B
finding: Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct zero-shot on ds-v5: counterfactual spec 24 (SFT-4B: 95), synthetic F1 88.7 (SFT: ~99), real F1 64.3 (SFT v1: 81). The big base teacher shares the frozen model's over-triggering failure — it never saw our policy or engineered hard boundaries, while the 4B was trained on exactly those. Bigger != better for a specialized policy task.
suggestions: Do NOT distill from a zero-shot larger Qwen — its quality ceiling is BELOW the specialized student, so OPD would drag the student toward the teacher's over-triggering (textbook teacher-dependence failure).
If OPD is still wanted: make the teacher competent first (few-shot/CoT prompt, or SFT the teacher), then distill; or accept that no readily-available teacher beats the SFT-4B here.

title: OPD (real run): distilling a weaker teacher regresses the student — not worth it here
finding: Ran the on-policy distillation loop for real: student=SFT-v2, teacher=Qwen3.5-27B, CoT rollouts on our prompts, 8 steps (teacher_kl ~0.1, stable). Result on ds-v5: F1 91.7->90.3, counterfactual spec 100->85.7 (pulled toward the teacher's over-triggering failure), synthetic 100->97.8, real 74->72, recall 99->95 — at ~15x completion tokens (CoT). No gains; mild regression on exactly the slices the teacher is weak on. More steps would pull harder toward the teacher.
suggestions: OPD is NOT worth it for this task: (1) the teacher quality-ceiling is below the specialized SFT student (f017), so KL drags it down; (2) the label output is ~1 token / short CoT, little to distill densely; (3) the residual gap is label ambiguity, not capacity. SFT (no-CoT) on ds-v5 is the production model.
OPD would only help with a teacher genuinely better than the student (e.g. a much stronger model, or the teacher itself SFT'd on this task) — none available here.

title: OPD with best teacher (3.6-27B): student regresses TOWARD the teacher's profile
finding: OPD v2 (teacher Qwen3.6-27B, 8 steps) on ds-v5: F1 90.3 (SFT v2: 91.7). The student moved toward the teacher's profile — real F1 74.4->77.5 (teacher's pii-spec 81 is relatively strong) but counterfactual spec 100->81 and synthetic 100->95.7 (teacher weak there). Net still below SFT, at ~15x cost. Clear illustration: distillation transfers the teacher's STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES; with a teacher below the student overall, the net is a loss.
suggestions: Final call: OPD is not worth it with any available zero-shot Qwen teacher. The only positive path would be to SFT the teacher first (capacity > student), then distill. SFT remains the production model.

title: Few-shot lifts the 27B teacher to parity AND makes it complementary (better PII)
finding: Qwen3.6-27B + 10 in-context examples (real-vs-placeholder, grant-vs-request, incident-vs-news, PII-record-vs-casual, obfuscated): F1 87.5->91.6, counterfactual spec 62->86, synthetic 92.8->97.8. Now ~parity with the SFT-4B (91.7) AND notably BETTER on PII spec (83 vs student's 52) — the student's residual weakness. First teacher with a strength the student lacks. Multi-teacher won't help (all teachers share the counterfactual weakness); few-shot is the cheap fix.
suggestions: OPD now has real signal to transfer (PII). Proper setup = ASYMMETRIC: teacher scores rollouts WITH few-shot prefix, student stays zero-shot (so the distilled student is good zero-shot). Needs a teacher-prefix hook in the OPD loop.
Alternative: SFT the 27B teacher (capacity > 4B) for unambiguous superiority, then distill.

title: Asymmetric few-shot OPD works mechanically — and proves OPD transfers strengths AND weaknesses
finding: Implemented asymmetric few-shot OPD (teacher scores rollouts with a 460-tok few-shot prefix; student stays zero-shot; monkeypatched incorporate_kl_penalty + logprob re-alignment). Student distilled from the few-shot 3.6-27B teacher (ds-v5): the teacher's PII strength DID transfer (pii spec 52->64, real F1 74->77), but global KL also imported its counterfactual weakness (spec 100->71). Net F1 89.1 < SFT 91.7.
suggestions: Definitive OPD read for this task: distillation is GLOBAL — it moves the student toward the teacher's whole profile. Net-positive OPD needs a teacher dominant on ALL axes (=> SFT the teacher) or SELECTIVE distillation (KL only on the slice the teacher wins, e.g. PII). With our teachers, SFT remains the production model.
Cheapest real win for PII was the rubric fix (f016), not OPD. If pursuing OPD: SFT-the-teacher, or per-slice KL weighting.

title: More OPD steps (8->32) do NOT help — dose-dependent convergence to the teacher profile
finding: 32-step asymmetric few-shot OPD vs 8-step: F1 89.1->89.3 (flat, still < SFT 91.7), but the profile shift is monotonic — pii spec 52->64->79 (toward teacher's 83), counterfactual spec 100->71->67 (toward teacher), teacher_kl 0.15->0.07 (converging). More distillation = stronger convergence to the teacher's whole profile, not a better model. Confirms OPD is profile-transfer: with no all-axis-superior teacher, no step count beats SFT.
suggestions: Stop tuning OPD steps — the lever moves the student along the SFT<->teacher line, and the teacher endpoint is not better overall. To actually win: a dominant teacher (SFT it) or selective per-slice KL (distill PII only). SFT v2 is the production model.

title: Native thinking (STaR rejection-sampling) underperforms direct SFT — coverage bias
finding: Trained Qwen3.5-4B on its OWN native <think> traces, keeping only correct ones (308/700 = 44% yield, renderer-trap-free raw-token SFT). Result on ds-v5: F1 84.2 (SFT no-CoT: 91.7), counterfactual spec 42.9 (~base level), real 65.7. Root cause: rejection sampling discards exactly the hard cases the frozen base gets WRONG — almost no correct counterfactual-negative traces are generated, so the thinking model never learns that discrimination (which direct SFT learns straight from labels). Also far less data (308 vs 2124).
suggestions: STaR can't teach what the base can't already sometimes do. To make thinking viable, generate traces from the SFT model (gets counterfactuals right -> correct hard-case traces), not the base. Even so, unlikely to beat direct SFT on this short task; the residual is label ambiguity, not reasoning.
Combined with f015 (templated CoT) and OPD (f018-f022): reasoning approaches don't help this short, decisive classification — direct-label SFT is best and cheapest.

title: Expanded real data (terraform/policy/Enron): new coverage + a generalization gap
finding: Added 3 HF sources -> ds-v6 (4009 rows): terraform_sec (real infra_config), infosec-policy docs (real security_policy, fills a synthetic-only gap), Enron email (real corporate negatives). The production SFT (trained on ds-v5) generalizes WELL to some: real security_policy F1 95, terraform infra_config spec 94 (correctly passes clean IaC). But real precision COLLAPSED to 33% (real F1 48.9) — it over-triggers on OOD negatives it never trained on (Enron email, PII prose). Note: real labeled data for secret_credential/access_control is scarce on HF (those aren't published) — confirms why we synthesize them.
suggestions: Two drivers of the precision drop: genuine OOD over-triggering AND Enron's conservative labeling (sensitive emails labeled negative -> some FPs are label noise). Retrain on ds-v6 to adapt to the new distribution; verify a sample of Enron 'FPs' to separate the two.
Caveat: 4 terraform 'secret' positives are regex FPs on 32-hex git/module hashes — tighten generic_hex_key to exclude path/hash contexts.

title: Retraining on ds-v6 closes the gap: more real data IN TRAINING restores precision
finding: SFT v3 (retrained on ds-v6) vs SFT v2 (ds-v5) on ds-v6: real F1 48.9->73.7, real precision 33->60, real spec 73->91; infra_config spec ->100, learned to pass Enron corporate email (spec 86), security_policy F1 95, synthetic still 100. The earlier collapse was an OOD distribution gap, not capacity — including the new real sources in training fixes it. SFT v3 is the new production candidate.
suggestions: Lesson: real data's value is highest IN TRAINING when it covers the deployment distribution; the held-out real eval reliably catches distribution gaps. Residual pii_handling over-trigger (spec 61) persists = the label-ambiguity floor (f016), not fixable by more data.

title: CoT SFT on ds-v6 confirms: not worth it (slightly worse, 15x cost)
finding: CoT SFT v3 (templated rationales, trained ds-v6) vs SFT v3 no-CoT on ds-v6: F1 86.5 vs 88.0, synthetic 97.8 vs 100, real 72.5 vs 73.7, counterfactual 96.3 vs 100; pii spec nudged up 61->67. Net slightly worse at ~15x completion tokens. Same verdict as f015 on the richer data.

title: 32-step few-shot OPD on ds-v6: FIRST competitive OPD — real F1 74->80, overall break-even
finding: OPD v5 (32-step, asymmetric few-shot Qwen3.6-27B teacher, from SFT v3) on ds-v6: overall F1 88.5 (vs SFT v3 88.0 — break-even/slight win), and clear gains where the teacher is strong: real F1 73.7->80.0, real precision 60->72, pii spec 61->72. The flip from ds-v5 (where OPD lost): ds-v6's realistic eval is real-heavy (487/659 rows), so the teacher's PII-discrimination strength transfers into the bulk of the eval and outweighs the counterfactual loss (only 47 synth rows). Trade unchanged though: counterfactual spec 100->57, synthetic 100->94, at ~38x completion tokens (CoT).
suggestions: Refined OPD verdict: it's worth it ONLY when (a) the teacher has a real strength the student lacks AND (b) that strength aligns with where your eval/deployment weight is. Here, optimizing for REAL precision, OPD v5 wins (real F1 80, best of any model); optimizing for counterfactuals or cost, SFT v3 wins. Pick by the operating point.
If shipping OPD v5: mitigate the counterfactual regression with selective per-slice KL (down-weight KL on counterfactual rollouts) or mix some counterfactual SFT loss in.

title: Label audit (regex+judge+Claude adjudication): kappa 0.906, ~1% true errors, 2 systematic sources
finding: scripts/audit_labels.py cross-checked 659 test labels vs regex + few-shot-27B judge: agreement 96.7%, Cohen's kappa 0.906 (high). 26 candidates flagged; I (Claude, independent) adjudicated all: ~6 genuine errors (~1%), ~8 contestable/rubric-dependent, ~12 label-correct (judge/regex over-triggered). Two SYSTEMATIC error sources found: (1) PII over-labeling on name/entity-heavy NARRATIVE (the >=3-identifier rule counts named entities, not sensitive identifiers — flags comments/strategy docs); (2) Enron conservative under-labeling missed real positives — incl. a real ACCESS GRANT and an actual disclosed PASSWORD labeled PASS.
suggestions: Fix PII rule: gate on SENSITIVE identifier types (SSN/card/license/ID/address+name combo), not raw entity count -> removes narrative false-positives.
Fix Enron labeling: run the judge (not metadata-only) to catch real positives (grants, password disclosures); conservative-negative default under-labels.
Resolve contestable rubric calls with stakeholders: docs/example keys, dead/revoked credentials, personal-contact disclosure in email.

title: Applied audit fixes (ds-v7): partial win + a methodology lesson on judge bias
finding: Fix 1: PII rule now gates on SENSITIVE/STRUCTURAL identifiers (address/phone/DOB/ID/financial), not bare names -> removed many narrative false-positives (PII pos 451->~390). Fix 2: password-disclosure regex (incl. JSON-quoted keys) -> secret_credential 8->39, catching real leaks the old regex missed. Re-audit on ds-v7: label-judge agreement DROPPED 96.7->95.2 / kappa 0.906->0.865 — but adjudication shows this is the JUDGE'S over-triggering bias (it still says TRIGGER on the narrative-PII we correctly flipped to PASS), NOT worse labels. Agreement with a same-family biased judge is not ground truth.
suggestions: Residual PII noise is largely IRREDUCIBLE from ai4privacy metadata: narrative with embedded name+date still slips through, and excluding generic DATE made a few DOB+phone records negative. Don't over-tune rules (diminishing returns + new errors); the honest path is a human-validated gold slice or an independent (non-Qwen) judge.
ds-v7 is a net label-quality improvement (clear narrative FPs removed + more real secret leaks caught); the residual is quantified and documented, not hidden.

title: SFT v4 on ds-v7: cleaner labels make the eval HONEST — PII is the true bottleneck
finding: SFT v4 (trained on cleaned ds-v7): synthetic 98.9, secret_credential 100, infra_config 100, security_policy 95.7 — excellent everywhere clean. But real F1 62.0 / precision 47.3, dominated by pii_handling (spec 51). This is LOWER than ds-v6 SFT v3 (real F1 73.7) NOT because the model regressed, but because ds-v6's lenient PII labels (narrative=positive) inflated real F1 by counting the model's PII over-triggers as correct. ds-v7's stricter/correct labels reveal the true PII over-triggering. Label quality changed the measurement, not the model.
suggestions: PII-prose is the irreducibly hard slice: both labels AND model struggle with 'how much PII = a sensitive record'. Real precision ~47 here is a LOWER bound (some FPs are residual label noise). Everything else (secrets/infra/policy/support/synthetic) is solved.

To move PII honestly: a human-labeled PII gold set + an explicit sensitivity threshold agreed with stakeholders; or route borderline PII to human review in production (high-recall model + human precision gate).

title: OPD v7 WINS on ds-v7: few-shot teacher fixes the PII bottleneck (revises the OPD verdict)
finding: 32-step asymmetric few-shot OPD (Qwen3.6-27B teacher) from SFT v4 on ds-v7: overall F1 80.8->85.7, real F1 62->74, real precision 47->65, pii spec 51->70 — the teacher's PII-discrimination strength directly fixes SFT's main weakness (PII over-triggering). Trade unchanged: counterfactual spec 100->57, synthetic 98.9->94.2, +45k completion tokens. This is the best model on the real/production distribution.
suggestions: REVISED OPD verdict: OPD is worth it when the teacher's strength aligns with the STUDENT'S bottleneck AND the eval's weight. ds-v5 (counterfactual-weighted) -> OPD lost; ds-v7 (PII-bottlenecked, correctly labeled) -> OPD clearly wins. The earlier 'not worth it' was distribution-specific, not universal.

Production: OPD v7 for real-precision priority (real F1 74, pii spec 70); SFT v4 for counterfactual robustness or low cost. Selective per-slice KL (skip counterfactual rollouts) would likely capture both.

title: Selective (real-only) OPD: partial — prompt selection can't fully isolate a slice
finding: OPD v8 distilled on REAL prompts only (counterfactuals never sampled), 32-step few-shot teacher from SFT v4. ds-v7: real F1 68 (SFT 62, global-OPD 74), pii spec 72 (transferred), counterfactual spec 76 (SFT 100, global-OPD 57), overall F1 83.7. So it HALVES the counterfactual damage and keeps the PII gain, but doesn't fully preserve counterfactual (76 not 100) and gives a smaller real gain. Mechanism: LoRA distillation updates SHARED weights, so a real-only rollout set still bleeds the teacher's distribution into counterfactual behavior — prompt selection alone can't isolate a slice.
suggestions: To truly get 'both', need a MIXED objective, not data selection: KL-to-teacher on real rollouts + an SFT/self-anchor loss on counterfactual rollouts (KL toward the frozen SFT student) to pin that slice. More cookbook surgery.
Production menu (ds-v7): SFT v4 (counterfactual-critical, cheap), OPD v8 (balanced: real 68 / cf 76), OPD v7 (max real precision 74, cf sacrificed).

title: Recipe transfers to Legal + Marketing via the per-domain spec (skill works)
finding: After refactoring to domains/<name>.py specs + shared --domain tools, built Legal and Marketing each as a spec + data/<name>/generate.py (same row schema + hard-family structure) in a fraction of the IT effort. Both run end-to-end through the unchanged eval harness: frozen Qwen3.5-4B F1 = Legal 99.1, Marketing 97.6 — sliceable per-subcategory/hardening, leakage-safe splits verified (train∩test=0). Both v1 SATURATE (as IT v0 did, f001) -> hardening is the documented next loop, same playbook. Domain character showed up: regex is empty/useless for Legal (boilerplate vs binding share legalese), weak for Marketing (superlatives appear in opinions too) — confirming regex anchors IT secrets but not Legal/Marketing semantics.
suggestions: Per-domain next steps mirror IT: harden counterfactuals until frozen F1 drops; add real HF sources (Legal: CUAD/contract corpora, EULA pages; Marketing: ad-copy/FTC/press); label-audit; SFT. Operating points differ: Legal favors RECALL (costly misses), Marketing/IT favor PRECISION.

title: Legal: the base model is genuinely strong — hardening doesn't create a gap
finding: Added subtler Legal counterfactuals (binding clause vs aspirational promise, our clause vs quoted-in-news, DPA vs privacy policy, executed vs textbook sample). Frozen Qwen3.5-4B F1 went 99.1 -> 100.0 (not down). The binding-instrument-vs-boilerplate/news/aspirational distinction is genuinely easy for the base model — unlike IT's over-triggering. Not every domain has a hard discriminative gap; manufacturing artificial difficulty would be hill-climbing noise.
suggestions: Legal needs little/no SFT (low training headroom). Spend effort on REAL legal data (CUAD/contracts, EULA pages) to test the synthetic->real transfer, not on more synthetic hardening. Auto-stop hardening after diminishing returns (supervisor retry cap).

title: Cross-domain: IT is hard for the base model; Legal & Marketing are largely solved zero-shot
finding: Hardening Marketing (claim-in-internal-note, opinion-with-superlative, competitor-claim, fact-phrased-as-claim) kept frozen F1 ~96 (cf spec 80->91). Combined with Legal (F1 100): the base Qwen3.5-4B is genuinely STRONG on Legal & Marketing, unlike IT where it over-triggered (frozen 75, cf-spec 24-62). So the recipe transfers cleanly but DIFFICULTY VARIES BY DOMAIN — the brief's 'hold up across distributions' answer is: same pipeline, very different headroom. IT needed heavy hardening + SFT; Legal/Marketing need little.
suggestions: Don't over-invest synthetic hardening/SFT where the base model is already strong. The open question for Legal/Marketing is REAL-WORLD transfer (synthetic->real), as it was for IT — build real fetchers (CUAD/contracts; ad-copy/FTC) to test it. Run one SFT each to confirm low headroom, then move to real.
